notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor, Battle of Chaffin’s Farm

narrative_blocks.header: A Medal of Honor outside Richmond
narrative_blocks.body: Fleetwood’s bravery at the Battle of Chaffin’s Farm in September 1864 earned the Medal of Honor, among the African American soldiers decorated for that single day’s fighting. He went on to clerk for the Freedmen’s Bureau and to organize Washington’s first Black National Guard unit.
He and Sara hosted weekly literary salons at their home in LeDroit Park, one of Black Washington’s intellectual centers. His papers went to the Library of Congress in 1947.

notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor, Battle of Chaffin’s Farm

narrative_blocks.header: A Medal of Honor outside Richmond
narrative_blocks.body: Fleetwood’s bravery at the Battle of Chaffin’s Farm in September 1864 earned the Medal of Honor, among the African American soldiers decorated for that single day’s fighting. He went on to clerk for the Freedmen’s Bureau and to organize Washington’s first Black National Guard unit.
He and Sara hosted weekly literary salons at their home in LeDroit Park, one of Black Washington’s intellectual centers. His papers went to the Library of Congress in 1947.

notable.notable_blurb: First Black nursing superintendent at Howard's Freedman's Hospital

narrative_blocks.header: First Black nursing superintendent at Freedman's Hospital
narrative_blocks.body: In 1901 she outscored all national applicants on the civil service examination to become the first Black nursing supervisor at Howard's Freedman's Hospital. The institution had been built into a center of Black medicine by Dr. Daniel Hale Williams, who taught her in Freedman's first nursing class.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife of a Medal of Honor recipient
narrative_blocks.body: She married Sgt. Major Christian Abraham Fleetwood in 1869 — recipient of the Medal of Honor for the Battle of Chaffin's Farm in 1864. Together they hosted weekly literary salons at 319 U Street in LeDroit Park, at the center of Washington's Black professional community.

Her great-grandfather Aaron Burr Jr. was the third Vice President; her grandmother Louisa Charlotte Burr was his unacknowledged daughter with Mary Emmons, confirmed by DNA in 2018. She grew up in Philadelphia among Webb cousins, including her uncle Frank J. Webb — author of The Garies and Their Friends.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded the Colored Women's League of Washington
narrative_blocks.body: In 1892 she was one of nine co-founders of the Colored Women's League of Washington. Her LeDroit Park home at 319 U Street is now a marker on the African American Heritage Trail.

She attended Oberlin College from 1856 to 1858 as a pupil-teacher — one of the few institutions then open to Black students. She went on to teach in Philadelphia, the Institute for Colored Youth, and schools in Frederick MD and Washington DC before entering nursing in 1893.

INST025.primary_name: Howard University / Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing
INST025.name: Howard University / Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing
INST025.notes:
Sara Louise Iredell Fleetwood (HD1935) — 8th-generation Hooker descendant and great-granddaughter of Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — enrolled in the first class admitted to Howard University's Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing in 1893, studying under Dr. Daniel Hale Williams (who had performed the first successful open-heart surgery the previous year). She graduated with Freedman's first graduating class in 1896 and in 1901 was appointed the first Black nursing superintendent at Freedman's Hospital, having outscored all national applicants on the civil service examination. Chief surgeon Dr. William A. Warfield gave her the title 'Directress of Nurses.' She organized the Freedmen's Nursing Association and served as the first Black woman on DC's nurse examining board (1907). Her husband Christian Fleetwood (Medal of Honor, Battle of Chaffin's Farm 1864) clerked at the Freedmen's Bureau after the Civil War. The Fleetwoods hosted weekly literary salons at 319 U Street NW — part of the African American Heritage Trail, Washington DC.
[hooker_connection] 8th-generation Hooker descendant served as first Black nursing superintendent
